name: Smallmouth bass

tactics.notes: Rocky points, gravel bars, current seams.
Streamers in current, poppers in calm water.

notes: Cool-water predator. In rivers, target current breaks and rocky structure.

name: Largemouth bass

tactics.notes: Weedlines, lily pads, submerged timber.

notes: Ambush predator — fish cover and structure. Slow presentations in cold water, faster in warm.

name: Walleye

tactics.notes: Troll along contour breaks at 12–25 ft.
Vertical jig over structure at dusk.

notes: Low-light feeder — dawn, dusk, and overcast days are best.

name: Northern pike

seasons.label: Open water; ice fishing also productive

tactics.notes: Steel leader is required — they bite through fluoro.

notes: Weed edges and cabbage beds. Aggressive ambush predator.

name: Lake trout

lures: Cowbells with sucker meat or Spin-N-Glo
tactics.notes: 60–120 ft on downriggers in summer; surface trolling in spring.
Drop to bottom over humps.

notes: Deep, cold structure. Summer fish push to 80–150 ft on the Great Lakes.

name: King (Chinook) salmon

seasons.label: Lake-Michigan run staging Aug → river runs Sep–Oct

tactics.notes: Great Lakes summer trolling — 60–120 ft.
River runs — drift through holding water.

notes: Manistee, Pere Marquette, Big Manistee see major fall runs. Float fishing with skein is highly productive.

name: Steelhead

tactics.notes: High-stick nymphing, indicator, or swung streamer.
Float-fishing with beads is the gold standard in MI.

notes: Manistee, Pere Marquette, Big Manistee, St. Joseph rivers in MI; tributaries to Lake Michigan / Lake Huron.

name: Brown trout

seasons.label: Open water — peak Hex hatch June

tactics.notes: Streamers for size, dries during a hatch.

notes: Even when nothing is hatching, big browns eat sculpins, crayfish, and other trout. Strip streamers along cut banks.

name: Rainbow trout

tactics.notes: Tailwater rainbows love midges and small mayflies.

notes: Caney Fork and South Holston rainbows feed on midges all winter.

name: Bluegill / panfish

tactics.notes: Cast a popper at sundown over a weed line — non-stop action.
Slow it down on the spawn beds.

notes: Great for kids and for warming up. Spawn beds are obvious in shallow water in June.

name: Crappie

tactics.notes: Vertical drop over brush piles.

notes: Suspended around timber and brush. Spider rigging works on big reservoirs.

name: Striped bass

tactics.notes: Cherokee, Tims Ford, Norris — troll deep water in summer.
Surface feeds at first/last light in fall.

notes: TN reservoirs hold massive stripers; cool tailwater outflows hold them through summer.

name: Redfish

tactics.notes: Sight-cast tailing fish on the low tide flats.
Work potholes and oyster bars on the falling tide.

notes: Time the falling tide on grass flats. The wake of a redfish in 8 inches of water is its own reason to fish.

name: Snook

seasons.label: Spring and summer in the passes; fall in backcountry

tactics.notes: Ambush points — mangrove roots, dock lights, passes.
Strip fast along mangrove edges.

notes: Closed season Dec–Mar (gulf) — check FWC rules before keeping any.

name: Spotted sea trout

tactics.notes: Grass flats, 3–5 ft of water, on a moving tide.

notes: Schoolies hit anything; gators (24+ in) are loners around structure.

name: Tarpon

tactics.notes: Lead the fish, strip-set, hold on.

lures: Live crab on the bridge tide
tactics.notes: Bridges, channels, passes.

notes: Catch-and-release only in FL. The fight of a lifetime.

name: Flounder

lures: Live mud minnow on jig head
tactics.notes: Slow drag along sandy bottom near structure.

notes: Inlets and channels in the fall — they stack up before moving offshore to spawn.

name: Channel catfish

seasons.label: Most active May–Sep, esp. after rain

tactics.notes: Bottom-fish holes, current seams, eddies after a fresh rain.

notes: The classic farm-pond fish. Big channels move into shallow water at night.

name: Bowfin (dogfish)

tactics.notes: Slow, stinky, weedy water.

notes: Tough fighters, ugly and prehistoric. Hand-of-the-bowfin caution — they bite hard.
